title: Sorrel drink
subtitle: Jamaican hibiscus, ginger, and spice drink
excerpt: Dried hibiscus calyces steeped overnight with fresh ginger, cinnamon, cloves, and allspice, then strained and sweetened to a deep crimson, tart, floral drink that tastes of nothing else. Sorrel (hibiscus) drink is the drink of the Jamaican Christmas season, made in large batches in December and served throughout the holidays. Spiked with rum or left unalcoholic, it is equally good in either form.
sourceNotes: Sorrel drink is made from the calyces of Hibiscus sabdariffa, known as sorrel in Jamaica and other parts of the Caribbean and as hibiscus flower in most of the world. The plant is grown throughout tropical regions and has been used for drinks in Jamaica since at least the 18th century. In Jamaica the sorrel harvest coincides with Christmas, making it the drink of the holiday season. The same drink appears as agua de jamaica in Mexico and as bissap in West Africa.

content.text: The steeping time is the most important variable. Eight hours produces a moderately flavoured drink; overnight (12 hours) produces the deep, full flavour of the traditional version. Do not boil the hibiscus, the colour leaches out and the flavour becomes bitter. Pour boiling water over the dried calyces and leave them to steep without further heat.

Sorrel drink can be served over ice as a soft drink, or spiked with rum at a ratio of roughly 1 part rum to 4 parts sorrel. The classic Jamaican Christmas version includes a generous measure of dark rum added after straining. Sugar can be added to taste, start with less than you think you need and adjust after straining and chilling.

Place the {{hibiscus-dried}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, {{cloves}}, and {{allspice}} in a large heatproof jug or pot. Pour over 1.5 litres of just-boiled water. Stir briefly and leave to steep, uncovered, for at least 8 hours or overnight. Strain through a fine sieve, pressing the solids gently to extract all the liquid. Add the {{caster-sugar}} and stir until dissolved. Taste and adjust sweetness. Add the {{rum-dark}} if using. Chill and serve over ice.

items.fix: Increase the hibiscus to 100 g and steep for 12 hours. The colour and flavour come from the time of contact
items.cause: Too little hibiscus or too short a steep

items.fix: Add more sugar gradually until the tartness balances. The sweetness level in the traditional version is quite high
Do not boil the hibiscus. Steep only in off-the-boil water, boiling extracts bitter compounds from the calyces

content.text: Sorrel drink is the taste of Jamaican Christmas. The hibiscus plant produces its calyces in late November and December in Jamaica, and the coincidence of harvest and holiday has made sorrel the Christmas drink for generations. In every Jamaican home in December, a batch of sorrel will be steeping, made more or less sweet, more or less spiced, more or less spiked depending on the household. In Britain the Jamaican diaspora makes sorrel drink at Christmas from dried hibiscus, keeping the tradition in a country where the plant does not grow.
